message:
feat(vfs): add asupersync io_uring backend, runtime disable-on-failure, and SHM lock fixes

This is a substantial change to the VFS layer covering three areas:

1. Asupersync io_uring backend (uring.rs):
   - Add a second io_uring backend behind the "linux-asupersync-uring"
     feature flag, powered by the asupersync crate. This enables A/B
     validation of io_uring backends. The existing uring-fs backend
     remains under the "linux-uring-fs" feature flag (still the default).
   - Both backends are mutually exclusive at compile time via cfg gates.
     A compile_error! is emitted if neither feature is enabled on Linux.
   - The asupersync backend opens files via AsupersyncIoUringFile which
     uses offset-based read_at/write_at operations, eliminating the need
     for seek+read/write sequences and the inode-level Mutex locking that
     the uring-fs backend requires. This is simpler and avoids the file
     position state management.
   - Add open_asupersync_backend() helper with test-only injectable
     failure via FORCE_ASUPERSYNC_INIT_FAIL atomic.

2. Runtime disable-on-failure mechanism (uring.rs):
   - Add an AtomicBool "disabled" flag to IoUringRuntime. When either
     backend encounters a panic or lock poisoning during I/O, the runtime
     permanently disables itself via disable(), and subsequent operations
     fall back to the unix VFS path. This prevents a single io_uring
     failure from repeatedly crashing the process.
   - The disable() method logs a warning on the first call and is
     idempotent on subsequent calls.
   - is_available() now checks both backend presence and the disabled
     flag.
   - For the uring-fs backend, add lock_mutex_or_io() helper that
     converts Mutex poison errors to io::Error instead of panicking,
     and is_lock_poison_error() to detect these errors for triggering
     the disable path.
   - Move the Vfs trait implementation block below the IoUringFile impl
     block for better code organization (the Vfs::open method now needs
     to reference asupersync-specific fields).

3. SHM locking fixes (unix.rs):
   - Remove unnecessary .into() calls on libc lock type constants
     (F_UNLCK, F_RDLCK, F_WRLCK). These were added for macOS aarch64
     compatibility but are not needed when the posix_lock function
     accepts i32 directly.
   - Fix a correctness bug in shm_lock exclusive re-acquisition: when a
     connection already holds the exclusive lock on a SHM slot
     (exclusive_owner == self.shm_owner_id), the code previously returned
     Ok(()) immediately without re-asserting the POSIX lock. This is
     wrong because another process could have released and re-acquired
     the lock in the meantime. Now re-assert the F_WRLCK POSIX lock and
     return Busy if it fails.
   - Add a path() accessor method on UnixFile gated behind the
     "linux-asupersync-uring" feature, needed by the asupersync backend
     to open its own file descriptor from the same path.

4. Test changes (unix.rs, uring.rs):
   - unix.rs: Restructure the WAL coordinator compatibility test to
     remove a racy SQLITE_BUSY assertion that depended on timing. The
     old test tried to write while the coordinator held the WAL write
     lock, but this was flaky. Instead, verify the write succeeds after
     release and assert the final row count is correct.
   - uring.rs: Add test_runtime_disable_is_sticky to verify the disable
     mechanism is permanent and idempotent.
   - uring.rs: Add test_lock_mutex_or_io_handles_poison_without_panicking
     (uring-fs only) to verify poisoned mutexes produce io::Error.
   - uring.rs: Add test_poisoned_runtime_falls_back_to_unix_path_and_disables_backend
     (uring-fs only) to verify end-to-end fallback after lock poisoning.
   - uring.rs: Add test_asupersync_init_failure_disables_backend_and_falls_back
     (asupersync only) to verify init failure triggers disable and
     subsequent I/O falls back to unix.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
